text: In my observation, there are two kinds of people around the web: Internet darlings who can keep converting the communication on the web into new values and then sometimes money by constantly updating themselves; and internet slaves who are just purposelessly strolling around the web and waste their time and even money sometimes.
correct: In my observation, there are two kinds of people on the web: Internet darlings who convert their communications over the web into new moral values and sometimes even money by keeping themselves constantly updated. Then there are Internet slaves who are purposelessly trolling around the web and waste their time and even money . ("Just" implies that one is on the edge of doing something or not doing something. It's difficult to "just" do something "on purpose". The author may have been watching CNN or Fox news because he or she likes to use these 'I'm-almost-sure' kind of qualifiers that make his or her words seem 'almost' untrue, or at the very least unsure. Rather than use 'sometimes', the author should see if he or she might be able to find a valid way of identifying the people whom he or she is addressing. He or she might consider that the latter group could be operationally defined as "obsessive" internet users, as an obsession can be defined as a behavior or habit that interferes with other parts of a person's life in undesirable ways. Finally, it confuses the reader to not know whether the author has or has not chosen to capitalize the word "Internet". He or she did so in the first case when inappropriate as by punctuation. He or she may want to use the lower-case for both; however, some people always capitalize the word Internet, due to an outdated manual's standard. About CNN and Fox: many critics of these stations cite their propensity for releasing stories in which they pack multiple qualifiers into the same sentence. For example, a recent report from CNN had a teleprompted reporter saying, "Who might these alleged victims possibly be?" Understanding the corporate structure of the news stations you watch might help the author to identify the people who are doing excellent reporting and the people who intentionally choose shoddy journalistic methods.)

text: The participants only have 1 euro a day, it actually means they ain't got no money (or ain't no money?
correct: The participants only have 1 euro a day, it actually means they ain't got no money (or ain't no money? "Ain't" is mostly used in the south-east US (often just called the South), so it sounds odd from anyone who isn't from there (even me, from Boston!). "Actually" has to mean that something is true. If they have 1 euro per day, it isn't true that they don't have any money. They have 1 euro! You can say, "In effect, it means that they have no money" or "They have almost/practically no money." Double-negatives (are not = ain't.... no) are not standard English, and only used in certain dialects like Southern US English.

The "so" tells readers that you're going to say something that contrasts with what you previously said, but is still a consequence of it. In a sentence "A, so B", B wouldn't be possible or make sense without doing/knowing A. I didn't pick "however", which is similar, because that doesn't imply consequence like "so" does.

In this case, easy has to be modified and have an -er added. When it's among 3+ things, then it's either "most or least + adjective" or "modified adjective + est". And it all needs to be capitalized because it is a proper noun in this case.) I really like studying there and learning new things about different countries and cultures.

text: In the meantime, I want to post a few photos from the Birthday Party, which took place last Sunday and which I visited as a guest.
correct: In the meantime, I want to post a few photos from a friend's birthday party last Sunday. (If you say "the Birthday Party" with the word "the" and capitalizing "Birthday Party" it sounds like an official event, like "the Linkin Park concert" or "the Presidential Election." So, unless this was a major/official event, then you need to use "a" instead of "the" and not capitalize the words. Another thing (sorry this is long...), if you don't say "a friend's birthday party" or "sister's birthday" or whatever, it can sound like you went to a stranger's birthday party.)
